coredata.dc:title: Promoting the Photo-Fenton catalytic activity with carbon dots: Broadening light absorption, higher applicable pH and better reuse performance
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yanqun
Guo, Pengran
Jin, Manyu
Gao, Ge
Xi, Qiaoyue
Zhou, Hua
Xu, Guobao
Xu, Jingwei
coredata.dc:description:
A new visible and near-infrared light-responsive heterogeneous Photo-Fenton catalyst, Fe3O4@Cu2O/carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) (FCCN) was prepared under mild conditions with the structure of double quantum dots growing on the surface of Fe3O4@Cu2O nanoparticles. The prepared FCCN could be used for the degradation of azo compounds, such as methyl orange, acid orange II and mordant yellow 10, even at neutral and alkaline pH (pH: 7–12) with a shortest complete degradation time of 15 min. During the Photo-Fenton process, CQDs and N-CQDs acting as up-converting photo-sensitizers improved the utilization of the light, in which the contribution of N-CQDs was greater than that of CQDs. Besides, CQDs and N-CQDs could transfer photo-generated charges between Cu2O and Fe3O4 for the rapid decomposition of H2O2 to
OH. CQDs as solid light-sensitive acid sites could consume OH− and enable the efficient decomposition of H2O2 under alkaline conditions. The magnetism of Fe3O4 made this catalyst easily separated and the insoluble layers of CQDs and N-CQDs allowed it to be repeatedly used without activity change even after 10 cycles. The degradation constant of methyl orange in FCCN/H2O2/light system is 5.4 times higher than that of the Fe3O4@Cu2O/H2O2/light system, indicating that the loaded quantum dots greatly enhanced the photocatalytic activity. The degradation constant in FCCN/H2O2/light system was 2.5 times higher than that of the simple mixture (Fe3O4@Cu2O + CQDs + N-CQDs)/H2O2/light system, which indicated these excellent catalytic performances should come from the synergism effect of all components in FCCN.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photo-Fenton reaction
Alkaline pH
Azo compound degradation
Up-conversion
Reusable catalyst

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province (grant numbers 2016B020240006 ) and Open Project Fund of Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Emergency Test for Dangerous Chemicals (grant numbers KF2017001 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the formation of FCCN and the digital photographs of FC, FCC, and FCCN powder.
(a) - (b) TEM image of FCCN. (c) – (e) HRTEM images of N-CQDs, CQDs and FCCN, respectively.
XRD patterns of CQDs, N-CQDs, fresh FCCN and used FCCN.
(a) Full scanned XPS spectrum of FCCN. (b) - (f) High-resolution XPS survey scan of Cu 2p, Fe 2p, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively.
N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms curves and pore-size distribution (inset) of (a) FC, (b) FCCN.

(a) The concentration (C/C0) changes and plots of –ln(C/C0) versus time (inset) for the degradation of MO in FCCN/H2O2/light system and FCCN/H2O2/dark system. (b) The up-converted PL spectra of CQDs aqueous solution. (c) Periodic ON/OFF photocurrent response in 0.25 M Na2S and 0.35 M Na2SO3 electrolyte (pH = 9.5) under visible light irradiation at -0.2 V vs. Ag/AgCl electrode. (d) Photocatalytic activities of FCCN and FC for MO degradation under light irradiation (λ ≥ 564 nm). (MO: 20 mg L−1, Cat.: 1 g L−1, H2O2: 15 mM, the initial pH = 7.0, unless otherwise specified).

(a) The concentration (C/C0) changes and plots of –ln(C/C0) versus time (inset) for the degradation of MO in FCCN/H2O2/light system and FCCN/light system. (b) Hydroxyl radicals trapping PL spectra in TA solution under light in presence of H2O2 under illumination at 24 min. (c) PL spectra of the powders of FC, FCC, FCN, and FCCN. (d) The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) Nyquist plots in the dark.

(a) Catalytic performance of FCCN, FCC, FCN and FC at pH = 9 (pH was adjusted by the addition of 0.1 M NaOH solution.). (b) pH value responses of different components and pH change versus time (inset) under light irradiation. (c) Catalytic performance of FCCN under different pH.
Catalytic performance of different catalysts on the PFR of MO degradation.
(a) Cycle test for the degradation of MO by FCCN. (b) Photocatalytic efficiency of the FCCN for the degradation of MO.
The UV–vis spectra of (a) AO II, (b) MY 10 degradation and (c) industrial wastewater (MO /AO II / MY 10 C0: 20 mg L−1, Cat: 1 g L−1, H2O2: 15 mM, the initial pH = 7.0).
The schematic diagram of catalytic mechanism.
Concentrations of Fe and Cu dissolved from FC and FCCN solution (1 g L−1) (obtained by Thermo X seriesⅡ ICP-MS).

head.ce:title: Promoting the Photo-Fenton catalytic activity with carbon dots: Broadening light absorption, higher applicable pH and better reuse performance

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, Jilin, 130022, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
A new visible and near-infrared light-responsive heterogeneous Photo-Fenton catalyst, Fe3O4@Cu2O/carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) (FCCN) was prepared under mild conditions with the structure of double quantum dots growing on the surface of Fe3O4@Cu2O nanoparticles. The prepared FCCN could be used for the degradation of azo compounds, such as methyl orange, acid orange II and mordant yellow 10, even at neutral and alkaline pH (pH: 7–12) with a shortest complete degradation time of 15 min. During the Photo-Fenton process, CQDs and N-CQDs acting as up-converting photo-sensitizers improved the utilization of the light, in which the contribution of N-CQDs was greater than that of CQDs. Besides, CQDs and N-CQDs could transfer photo-generated charges between Cu2O and Fe3O4 for the rapid decomposition of H2O2 to
OH. CQDs as solid light-sensitive acid sites could consume OH− and enable the efficient decomposition of H2O2 under alkaline conditions. The magnetism of Fe3O4 made this catalyst easily separated and the insoluble layers of CQDs and N-CQDs allowed it to be repeatedly used without activity change even after 10 cycles. The degradation constant of methyl orange in FCCN/H2O2/light system is 5.4 times higher than that of the Fe3O4@Cu2O/H2O2/light system, indicating that the loaded quantum dots greatly enhanced the photocatalytic activity. The degradation constant in FCCN/H2O2/light system was 2.5 times higher than that of the simple mixture (Fe3O4@Cu2O + CQDs + N-CQDs)/H2O2/light system, which indicated these excellent catalytic performances should come from the synergism effect of all components in FCCN.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, more and more artificial dyes are synthesized to meet people’s demand for the varied colours. Because of dye’s properties, such as the toxicity, anti-photolysis, anti-oxidation, and anti-biodegradation, the direct discharge of organic dyes in the wastewater produces a serious threat to water resources and living environment [1,2]. Since up to 70% of organic dyes are azo compounds [3–6], the degradation methods and materials for azo compounds are urgently needed for the environmental protection.

Fenton’s reaction is widely used in the treatment of wastewater. In the redox cycle of iron, H2O2 produces highly active hydroxyl radicals (•OH, E0 = 2.80 V/SHE) for degradation of stubborn pollutants [7]. The degradation products with lower molecular weight are easily biodegradable [8]. At present, common Fenton’s reactions include traditional Fenton reactions (Fe2+/ H2O2 system) and Fenton-like reactions (H2O2+Fe2+-solid/Fe3+-solid) [9]. However, there are some drawbacks in traditional homogeneous Fenton’s reactions. First, the concentration of Fe2+ (50–80 ppm) in solution required for the reaction is far beyond the allowable emission limit (2 ppm) set by the European Union [8,10]. Second, the Fe2+/H2O2 system works normally under acidic conditions (pH: 2–4) but in many real cases the pH of the wastewater is neutral or alkaline [8,11,12]. What’s more, in Fe2+/H2O2 system, Fe (II) is difficult to reuse and produces large amounts of sludge [13,14]. These shortcomings restrict the application of traditional Fenton’s reactions on a large scale.

In order to overcome these drawbacks, the Photo-Fenton reaction (PFR), which utilizes light to further promote the reaction, draws more and more attention. So far, many heterogeneous photo-Fenton catalysts have been reported, for instance, ZnFe2O4 [3], C3N4/WO3 [15], TiO2/Fe2TiO5/Fe2O3 [16], Fe(III)/CQDs/Fe-doped g-C3N4 [17], Fe3O4/MWCNTs [18], γ-Fe2O3/SiO2 [19], α-FeOOH/MesoC [20], Fe2O3/g-C3N4 [21], CdS-carbon nanotube/TiO2 [22]. The cycle ability and catalytic efficiency of these composites have been greatly improved. Although there is a great progress in the field of PFR, it is still a challenge to develop a catalytic system that works well under pH > 7, uses visible and near-infrared light, and can be recycled many times.

Recently, due to the good biocompatibility, low toxicity, and excellent up-conversion photoluminence property [23–28], more and more composites composed of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and semiconductors are prepared and used in the photo-catalysis field [29–34]. Among them, for example, CQDs/TiO2 [30], N-CQDs/TiO2 [31], the mesoporous hematite nanospheres embedded with CQDs [32], CQDs/Bi2WO6 [33], CQDs/Ag3PO4 [34], these materials with CQDs have enhanced catalytic activity for the degradation of organic pollutants by broadening the light absorption range and improving charge separation and transfer. In the photo-degradation process of semiconductor catalytic systems, the active species are mainly •O2 − (E0=-0.33 V/SHE) and h+ of which the oxidation capacities are significantly weaker than that of •OH. So most semiconductor catalytic systems are time-consuming, and couldn’t completely degrade stubborn pollutants.

Based on the excellent performances, we use CQDs to promote photo-Fenton reaction, which is expected to overcome the shortcomings of the Fenton reaction. Herein, we report a type of heterogeneous photo-Fenton catalyst Fe3O4@Cu2O/CQDs/ nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) (FCCN), which exhibits excellent catalytic and reusable ability for degradation of azo dyes in neutral and alkaline solutions under irradiation. In this work, the morphologies, structures, optical properties, photo-catalytic properties, alkali tolerance and reused performances of the composite FCCN are investigated in detail. The photo-catalytic properties of FCCN are evaluated by the degradation of three kinds of dyes and actual industrial wastewater. The relationship between the enhanced photo-catalytic activities and the carbon dot-based structure is discussed.

Copper(II) nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, 99%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 96%), and absolute ethanol (EtOH, 99.7%), ethylene glycol (99.5%), glucose (C6H12O6), and urea (99%) were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Calcium citrate (99%), methyl orange (MO, 96%), acid orange II (AO II, 85%), mordant yellow 10 (MY 10, 99%), and Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP 30 K, 98.5%) were purchased from Aladdin. Iron oxide (Fe3O4, 99%, 30 nm, Figure S1a) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The industrial wastewater sample was collected from Guangdong Institute of Analysis. All chemicals were used directly without further purification.

FC was prepared according to our previous report with minor modification [35]. A general procedure as following: Cu(NO3)2·3H2O (0.4832 g, 2 mmol) was added into a mixed solvent of ethylene glycol (17 mL) and EtOH (34 mL) with stirring at room temperature for 5 min. Then Fe3O4 nanoparticles (50 mg) were added into the mixed solution and then sonicated for 20 min to get finely dispersed Fe3O4 solution. Subsequently, the mixed solution was transferred into a teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (100 mL capacity), placed in a 170℃ oven for 6 h, and then cooled to room temperature naturally. Finally, the products were separated by a magnet and thoroughly washed with ethanol and deionized water (DI water) several times and dried under vacuum.
Pure Cu2O was prepared by the same method as the preparation of FC except without the addition of Fe3O4.

CQDs were prepared according to a previous report with minor modification [36]. Firstly, NaOH solution (50 mL, 1 M) was added into glucose solution (50 mL, 1 M), then the mixture solution was sonicated for 3 h so that the colour of the mixture was gradually changed to brown. The CQDs were purified by dialysis (the dialysis membrane: 500 Da).

N-CQDs were prepared according to a previous report with minor modification [37]. Firstly, urea (3.0 g) and calcium citrate (3.0 g) were dissolved in 10 mL DI water, and then heated in a microwave oven for 6 min obtaining a yellow honeycomb solid. Subsequently, the solid N-CQDs were dissolved in 50 mL DI water, and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min to remove unreacted residues. Finally, the upper yellow solution was dialyzed using a dialysis bag (500 Da).

FC (0.5 g) was added into a conical flask containing 150 mL NaOH (1 M) aqueous solution and the mixture was given a 10 min ultrasonic treatment. Subsequently, 20 mL of PVP solution (50 g•L−1) was injected and 100 mL glucose solution (1 M) was slowly added into the above mixture. Then the conical flask was treated with ultrasonic for another 20 min. Subsequently, the mixture was aged for 16 h. Finally, the precipitate was washed and dried.

FC (0.5 g) was added into a conical flask containing N-CQDs (50 mL) solution. Then the conical flask was treated with ultrasonic for 10 min. Subsequently, the mixture was aged for 16 h. Finally, the precipitate was washed and dried.

First, FCC was prepared according to the above method (2.2.5), and then the N-CQDs were grown on its surface according to the preparation of FCN (2.2.6). Finally, the products were thoroughly washed and dried.

The sample morphologies were studied by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) on a Philips-FEI XL 30-SFEG at an accelerating voltage of 20 kV and without Au coating. The elemental distribution of the composite was characterized by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) attached to the XL-30 SEM. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) were conducted on a JEM-2100 F high-resolution TEM operating at 200 kV. The crystal structure of the sample was obtained by Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) on a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation and the sample was scanned from 0° to 90° in 2θ. The absorption spectra of the azo dyes solution were collected on a Shimadzu UV-3101PC UV–vis spectrophotometer from 300 nm to 800 nm at room temperature. The intensity of the absorbance peak at 464 nm was used to assess the concentration of MO remaining in the solution according to the Lambert-Beer’s law: A = a (λ) * b * c, where A was the intensity of the measured absorbance, a (λ) was a wavelength-dependent absorptivity coefficient, b and c was the path length and the analyte concentration, respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained using a thermo ESCALAB 250 spectrometer equipped with Al Kα X-ray source. Electrospray ionization mass spectrometry (ESI-MS) was used to analyze the residual dye and degradation intermediates of MO, AO II, and MY 10. Fluorescence spectra were measured using a RF5301 PC fluorescence spectrometer. Electrochemical properties were measured by a CHI660E electrochemical workstation with Pt wire as counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl electrode as reference electrode. Working electrodes were fabricated by coating samples onto an ITO (8mm*5 cm) substrate covered by a carefully tailored electroplating tape with a hole ( ϕ = 4.5 m m ) on it.

MO, a common azo dye, was selected as the model contaminant to assess the photocatalytic activity of these prepared samples. The light source was a 500 W xenon lamp and kept 15 cm over the photocatalytic reactor. Each sample was dispersed into MO solution (50 mL, 20 mg L−1) and the mixture was placed 60 min under dark to reach the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Prior to illumination, a specific dose of H2O2 was added to the above mixture. Then the suspension solution was immediately exposed to light. All tests were carried out at room temperature. At a certain time interval, 3 mL solution was collected and the heterogeneous catalyst was removed from solution using a magnet. Subsequently, the intensity of the absorbance peak of the obtained supernatant was measured at wavelength of 464 nm by a spectrophotometer.

The relative amounts of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) generated by the various catalysts in the PFR were estimated by a terephthalic acid (TA) alkaline solution (TA: 2 × 10−3 M, NaOH: 1 × 10−2 M) under illumination. 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (TA-•OH) formed by the reaction of •OH with TA (Eq (1)) could emit fluorescence at 426 nm under the excitation of 312 nm light [38]. The difference of fluorescence intensity at the same time could reflect the amount of •OH generated.
(1)
O
H •
+ T A → T A O H

The fabrication process of FCCN composite was illustrated in Scheme 1 . Firstly, tremella-like FC spheres (Figure S1b) were prepared through the heterogeneous nucleation process. FCC (Figure S1c) and FCCN (Figure S1d) were successively obtained from the CQDs and N-CQDs solution. The prepared FC, FCC, FCCN powder were khaki, grayish black, and black, respectively, suggesting a progressively enhanced visible light absorption capacity. The morphology and nanostructure of FCCN were investigated via SEM, TEM and HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a and b, the composite had a rougher surface with point-like particles on the edge; the diameter of FCCN was 2–3 μm. The HRTEM images of point-like particles showed well-resolved and characteristic lattice fringes (Fig. 1c and d). The particles with a lattice fringes spacing of 0.23 nm, corresponding to the (100) planes of graphene, were CQDs [24]. The particles with a lattice fringes spacing of 0.21 and 0.34 nm, corresponding to the (100) and (002) planes of nitrogen-doped graphene, were N-CQDs [39]. In addition, the structure of the subsurface was well resolved in Fig. 1e. The lattice spacing of 0.26 and 0.15 nm corresponded to the (311) and (440) planes of Fe3O4, respectively (JCPDF: 75-0033). The lattice spacing of 0.25 nm corresponded to the (111) planes of Cu2O [40]. It was obvious that CQDs and N-CQDs attached directly to the surface of Fe3O4 and Cu2O (Fig. 1e). The TEM and HRTEM results indicated the successfully presence of CQDs and N-CQDs in the FCCN. That was further confirmed by the EDX analysis (Figure S2). What’s more, corresponding elemental mappings (Figure S3) showed that various elements in the catalyst were uniformly distributed.

The crystal textures of samples were characterized via XRD (Fig. 2 ). The diffraction peak at 9.17° and 21.5° corresponded to the (001) planes of CQDs/N-CQDs and the (002) planes of N-CQDs, respectively, which matched well with the values reported in the literature [41,42]. The well-defined peaks of FCCN in XRD pattern were ascribed to the facets of Cu2O, Fe3O4 and CQDs, which were consistent with the HRTEM results. In theory, the XRD diffraction peaks of the fresh FCCN should be the sum of those of the four components. The disappearance of the peaks of N-CQDs may be due to the low content and high dispersion of N-CQDs in the composite.

The FCCN composite was also characterized by XPS. The full-range spectrum of FCCN (Fig. 3 a) clearly indicated the presence of Cu, Fe, C, N, and O in the sample. The surface characteristics were further investigated via high-resolution XPS spectra. The details of high-resolution peaks of each element were presented in the Fig. 3b-f. The binding energy of Cu 2p was assigned to Cu 2p3/2 (932.4 eV) and Cu 2p1/2 (952.3 eV), indicating that copper was in the form of Cu2O [43]. And the observed satellite peak at 944.5 eV indicated the existence of Cu2O-O bond with excellent stability in the composite [44]. The high-resolution spectrum of the Fe 2p3/2 was composed of two peaks at 711.0 eV and 713.8 eV, corresponding to the Fe2+ and Fe3+ [18], which was consistent with the XRD result of Fe3O4. The peaks of C 1s for CC, C–H, C–O/C-N, and OC = O were also observed in the XPS spectrum [37,45–47]. The binding energy of N 1s was fitted into two peaks at 398.3 eV and 400.0 eV, which were pyridinic N and pyrrolic N [48], suggesting the presence of N-CQDs on the composite surface. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of the O 1s exhibited three peaks, which were attributed to Cu-O, CO and COC, respectively [37]. Compared to the XPS spectra of FC (Figure S4), the corresponding peaks of the FCCN shifted about 0.3 – 0.6 eV to higher binding energies, indicating the change in the chemical surroundings of the Fe3+, Fe2+, and Cu+ ions after the load of CQDs/N-CQDs and N-CQDs on the FCCN surface [49]. That may be because Fe3+, Fe2+, and Cu+ ions had a coordinative interaction with the residual oxygen functionalities (such as carboxylic acid and hydroxyl) of CQDs/N-CQDs and N-doped sites of N-CQDs [50]. Simultaneously, the coordinative interaction between the various components increased the interaction between these different materials and created a large number of interfaces for charge transfer [50,51] that resulted in a quenched PL intensities of powder samples and increased charge transfer rates, which could be seen later.

The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm curves and pore characteristics of the prepared samples were provided in Fig. 4 . The specific Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of FC decreased from 133.432 m2 g−1 to 51.797 m2 g−1 after decorated by CQDs and N-CQDs, which might be attributed to that some small-sized pathways were blocked by quantum dots. As a result, the pore diameter of the composite increased to 3.334 nm. The larger diameter of the pores might be more beneficial for the transfer of the desorbed degradation products.

The degradation of MO was selected to assess the catalytic performance of the composite. Figure S5 showed the removal of MO as a function of the time under different conditions. It could be observed that MO could hardly be degraded without catalysts, light irradiation or H2O2. When FCCN, H2O2 and light were present simultaneously, the degradation rate of MO was rapid, which showed that FCCN, H2O2 and light were necessary.

The degradation constant of MO in FCCN/H2O2/light system (κ = 0.222) was much higher than that of the FCCN/H2O2/dark system (κ = 0) (Fig. 5 a), which indicated that the light irradiation was very important for the degradation of MO. As we all know, Cu2O (band gap = 2.2 eV) could be excited only by light with a wavelength shorter than 564 nm. While CQDs and N-CQDs had excellent up-conversion property, they could be excited by long-wavelength light (≥ 564 nm) to produce up-converted emissions in the range of 310 to 564 nm and 330 to 564 nm, respectively (Fig. 5b and Figure S6). This suggested that the up-conversion performances of CQDs and N-CQDs might make FCCN use more visible and near-infrared (NIR) light [40]. The maximum up-conversion quantum yields of CQDs and N-CQDs were 8.2% and 7.0% (Figure S7), respectively, which were measured via the comparative method proposed by Williams et al [52]. To further identify the catalytic improvement came from the up-conversion property, we detected the photo-generated charge intensity of different samples under the visible light. Fig. 5c showed that photocurrent intensity of FCCN presented the highest value which was almost 1.17, 1.85 and 3.07 folds of FCN, FCC and FC, respectively, indicating the higher photocatalytic activity. The contribution of up-conversion property to photocatalytic activity was also informed by contrast experiments under the filter which only retained the light with a wavelength greater than 564 nm (Fig. 5d). Compared with FC, the photocatalytic activity of FCCN was stronger. Due to the load of CQDs and N-CQDs, FCCN could still achieve the complete degradation of MO, while FC could only degrade 28% within 60 min, and the degradation constant of MO in the FCCN system was 6 times of that in the FC system under visible and near-infrared light (≥ 564 nm) (Fig. 5d inset). However, the improvement of photocatalyst activity was not caused solely by the up-conversion properties. The introduction of CQDs and N-CQDs also promoted the separation and transfer of photo-induced carriers, which could be seen later.

The degradation constant of MO in FCCN/H2O2/light system (κ = 0.222) was much higher than that of the FCCN/light system (κ = 0.006) (Fig. 6 a), which indicated that the active groups were mainly coming from the decomposition of H2O2. We detected the concentration of •OH in the photo-Fenton reaction process by using a terephthalic acid probe which produced the stronger intensity of fluorescence with the higher concentration of •OH. (Fig. 6b). As expected, the fluorescence intensity in FCCN, FCC, FCN, FC system was weakened in turn, and the fluorescence intensity of FCCN system is the strongest, which further confirmed that the presence of CQDs and N-CQDs in FCCN contributed to the decomposition of H2O2 to •OH. The powder samples loaded with different quantum dots were characterized by PL spectra. The PL intensity decreased gradually in the order of FC, FCC, FCN and FCCN (Fig. 6c), indicating the increasing efficiency of charge separation [53]. In addition, we had performed other electrochemical characterization to further investigate the separation of photo-generated charge carrier. As shown in the Fig. 6d, the semicircular Nyquist plots of sample obeyed the order of FCCN < FCN < FCC < FC, also suggesting the more efficient separation and transfer of the photo-induced carriers after the load of CQDs and N-CQDs [51]. Herein, we concluded that CQDs and N-CQDs could transfer the photo-generated charges between Cu2O and Fe3O4, which could improve the circulation of Fe3+/Fe2+ for the efficient decomposition of H2O2 to •OH.

The common Fenton’s reaction was only suitable for acidic conditions [54,55], while most wastewater was neutral or alkaline. Therefore, the pH was of great significance for the applicability of Fenton’s reaction. Here, the effect of pH on the degradation rate was investigated. When pH = 9, compared with FC and FCN, FCC and FCCN still had superior catalytic activity (Fig. 7 a), which reflected FCC and FCCN had good tolerance to alkali. Fig. 7b showed the ability to reduce pH of the aqueous solution of each component under illumination (△pHCQDs = -1.21, △pHN-CQDs ≅ -0.3, △pHFC ≅ 0.09, △pHFCCN = -0.76, △pHFCCN+MO = -0.62), which suggested the pH reduction in the FCCN/H2O2/light system under illumination depended mainly on the presence of CQDs [56] and the degradation process of MO consumed H+. Subsequently, the applicable pH range of FCCN/H2O2 system was investigated (Fig. 7c). When pH = 7∼11, the degradation rate of FCCN/H2O2 system was very fast and the system could completely degrade MO within 70 min. When pH = 12, with the addition of H2O2 for several times, the complete degradation of MO could also be achieved. With the increase of pH, H2O2 was more easily decomposed into HOO− and O2, so the generation of •OH became more difficult (Eqs. (2) and (3)) [57,58]. In order to produce enough •OH, it is necessary to add more H2O2.
(2)
H 2
O 2
+ O
H −
→ H O
O −
+
H 2
O
(3)
H 2
O 2
+ H O
O −
→
H 2
O + O
H −
+
O 2

The catalytic activity of various components of the FCCN was shown in Fig. 8 . With the increase of illumination time, CQDs and FC systems tended to stop degrading MO. However, the concentration of MO in the N-CQDs/H2O2 system had been decreasing until MO was completely degraded. In addition, FCC and FCN could make the degradation of MO not only complete but also rapid, and FCCN made the catalytic performance more superior. The degradation constant of MO in the FCCN/H2O2 system was 5–37 times higher than that of the single component (CQDs, N-CQDs or FC)/H2O2 system (Fig. 8 inset). The catalytic performance of a photo-catalyst was closely related to the specific surface area, crystal structure, light absorption range, the separation and transport of charge and the effective utilization of H2O2 [59,60]. Generally, the larger specific surface area, the higher catalytic activity. However, the BET results showed that the specific surface areas of FCCN and FC were 51.797 m2 g−1 and 133.432 m2 g−1, respectively, which suggested that the higher catalytic activity of FCCN didn’t rely on its specific surface area. From XRD pattern (Fig. 2 and Figure S9), FCCN had the same crystal structure as FC skeleton, which indicated that the change of catalytic activity was independent of the crystal structure. Based on the experimental results and evidence, we deduced that CQDs and N-CQDs could make the composite utilize more visible and N-IR light, and increase the efficiency of charge separation. CQD releasing protons under illumination to neutralize OH− anions in solution improved the effective decomposition of H2O2. What’s more, when a simple mixture of FC + CQDs + N-CQDs served as catalyst, its degradation constant was also lower than of the FCCN/H2O2/light system. Overall, the enhanced photocatalytic activity of FCCN should come from the synergism effect of all components in FCCN.

The reusability of the catalyst was of great importance in industrial applications. The reusability of the FCCN was tested as shown in Fig. 9 a. The catalytic activity did not change after recycled 10 times (Fig. 9b). The concentrations of Fe and Cu elements dissolved from FC and FCCN were measured by ICP-MS. As shown in Table 1 , the amount of dissolved Fe and Cu in the solution of the FCCN system was less than that of FC, which suggested the introduction of double quantum dots was beneficial for the stability of the composite. In addition, comparing XRD patterns (Fig. 2) and XPS (Figure S10) spectra of the catalyst before and after reusing, there were almost no change about the crystal structure and composition, also suggesting that the catalyst could be used repeatedly.

FCCN was used to degrade two other azo dyes, (AO II and MY 10) and actual industrial wastewater (Fig. 10 a–c). During the degradation of AO II (Fig.10a), the CQDs would emit hydrogen protons (H+) under illumination so that AO II could change into its isomers making the conjugation system larger, which was beneficial for easier electronic transition [61,62]. As a result, the red-shifted absorption peaks of AO II appeared. Subsequent decline of the absorption peak was due to the destruction of the chromophore and the formation of colourless products. Figure S11 and Figure S12 were the mass spectra of degradation products of AO II and MY 10 under different illumination time. The degradation products had a smaller molecular weight than the original dyes. The test of the photocatalytic performance of FCCN was also carried out to degrade actual colorless industrial wastewater (Fig. 10c). Within 60 min, the absorption peak of the contaminant gradually decreased until disappeared, suggesting that the colourless contaminant could also be destroyed.

The ESI-MS was used to determine the degradation intermediates (Figure S13). Before illumination, a set of isotope peaks of MO (M/Z = 304) indicated and only MO was present in the aqueous solution. When light irradiation time reached 3 min, the peaks of MO still existed, but some new fragment ion peaks (such as M/Z = 290, 276, 218, 198, 170, 157, 112, and 97) appeared, implying that some new intermediates with lower molecular weight appeared and the structure of MO was partially cracked. After 10 min, the peaks of MO disappeared, and only the peaks of some lower molecular weight substances were presented, which indicated that MO was completely degraded. Figure S14 showed the possible intermediate products in the degradation process of MO.

Based on the above experimental results and discussion, we proposed following mechanism for the enhanced Photo-Fenton catalytic activity (Fig. 11 ). Firstly, the up-conversion characteristic of CQDs and N-CQDs improved the light utilization of FCCN. Secondly, the load of CQDs and N-CQDs could separate and transfer photo-generated charges between Fe3O4 and Cu2O more efficiently, accelerating the circulation of Fe3+/Fe2+ for the decomposition of H2O2 to active species •OH. Thirdly, the light-induced protons of CQDs could change the partial pH, so that the FCCN system could work in alkaline solution. Finally, the insoluble layers of CQDs and N-CQDs were beneficial to enhance the stability of the composite. And there was cooperative interaction between each component, so the synergism from all the components of FCCN enhanced the photocatalytic properties.

A novel visible and near-infrared light-responsive heterogeneous catalyst, FCCN was prepared under mild conditions with the structure of double quantum dots growing on the surface of Fe3O4@Cu2O nanoparticles. The photocatalytic activity of this catalyst was investigated using methyl orange, acid orange II, mordant yellow 10, and industrial wastewater. The azo dye MO was completely degraded in the shortest degradation time of 15 min. The CQDs and N-CQDs not only made the composite material respond to visible light and near-infrared light, but also acted as a ‘bridge’ to transfer the photo-generated charges of Cu2O to Fe3O4, improving the circulation of Fe3+/Fe2+ for the efficient decomposition of H2O2 to •OH. The magnetism of Fe3O4 made this catalyst easily separated and the insoluble layers of CQDs and N-CQDs made the catalyst reusable. The degradation constant of methyl orange in FCCN/H2O2/light system was 5–37 times higher than that of the single component (CQDs, N-CQDs or Fe3O4@Cu2O)/H2O2/light systems, and was 2.5 times higher than that of the simple mixture (Fe3O4@Cu2O + CQDs + N-CQDs)/H2O2/light systems which suggested that these excellent catalytic performances should come from the synergism effect of all components in FCCN.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A practical and reusable catalyst for the synthesis of donor-acceptor cyclopropane, Catal. Commun.
